title: Faith, Testimony, and Assurance
subtitle: Victory through faith, the threefold witness, confidence in eternal life

heb.gloss: to beget, bear, give birth to
heb.paragraph: Everyone who believes that Jesus is the Christ has been born of God - faith is evidence of divine birth, not its cause.
This is the victory that has overcome the world - our faith - faith is the instrument of victory over the world system.
Who is it that overcomes the world except the one who believes Jesus is the Son of God - faith in Jesus identity is world-conquering power.

refs.note: In the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world.
My yoke is easy, and my burden is light.
To the one who conquers I will grant to eat of the tree of life.
In all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us.

notes.note: Everyone believing Jesus is the Christ has been born of God. Everyone who loves the Father loves whoever has been born of him.
Love for God means keeping his commandments - and they are not burdensome. Everyone born of God overcomes the world. This is the victory: our faith.
Calvin notes the logical chain: faith evidences new birth; new birth produces love for God siblings; love for siblings proves love for Father.
Faith overcomes by connecting us to the One who has already overcome. Our victory is participation in his victory.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Has been born (gegenneetai) is perfect tense - prior completed action. Faith reveals what God has already done. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

Has overcome (nikesasa) is aorist - victory viewed as accomplished fact. Believers share Christ decisive victory. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

yarbrough.source: Robert Yarbrough, BECNT 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Yarbrough emphasizes that faith is not self-generated power but connection to Christ power. We overcome by believing in the Overcomer. Yarbrough traces the epistle's logic through its characteristic contrasts: light and darkness, truth and falsehood, love and hate. The author's binary rhetoric is not simplistic but diagnostic — it exposes the fundamental orientation of the heart and calls readers to genuine self-examination in the light of God's revealed character.

kruse.source: Colin Kruse, TNTC/Pillar 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kruse notes that commandments are not burdensome because the Spirit enables obedience. Love transforms duty into delight. Kruse's analysis focuses on the letter's pastoral function within its historical context. The author addresses concrete community concerns — false teaching, relational fracture, assurance of salvation — with theological precision that grounds pastoral care in the foundational realities of the incarnation and atonement.

hist.historical: First John addresses a crisis caused by secessionists who have left the Johannine community (2:19). These opponents likely held proto-Gnostic views: denying that Jesus Christ came 'in the flesh' (4:2), claiming sinless perfection while living immorally (1:8-10), and failing to love fellow believers (2:9-11). The author, traditionally identified as the apostle John writing from Ephesus in the 90s AD, establishes tests of authentic Christianity: right belief about Christ's incarnation, righteous living, and love for the community. The letter's repetitive, spiral structure reinforces these themes through variation rather than linear argument. The community had experienced a traumatic split, and the letter reassures those who remained of their standing with God.
hist.context: Everyone who believes that Jesus is the Christ has been born of God, and everyone who loves the Father loves whoever has been born of him. By this we know that we love the children of God, when we love God and obey his commandments. For this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments. And his commandments are not burdensome. For everyone who has been born of God overcomes the world. And this is the victory that has overcome the world - our faith. Who is it that overcomes the world except the one who believes that Jesus is the Son of God?

heb.paragraph: This is he who came by water and blood - water refers to Jesus baptism, blood to his death. Both are essential.
There are three that testify: the Spirit, the water, and the blood - the threefold witness confirms Christ identity.
God gave us eternal life, and this life is in his Son - eternal life is not merely duration but quality of relationship.

refs.note: One of the soldiers pierced his side, and at once there came out blood and water.
Jesus was baptized and the Spirit descended - the baptism testimony.
When the Helper comes, he will bear witness about me.
Whoever believes in the Son has eternal life.

notes.note: Jesus came by water and blood - not water only but water and blood. The Spirit testifies because the Spirit is truth. Three witnesses agree.
God testimony is greater than human testimony. Whoever believes has the testimony in himself. God gave us eternal life; this life is in his Son.
Calvin interprets water and blood as baptism and death - both are necessary. The false teachers may have accepted baptism but denied atoning death.
Life is in the Son, not apart from him. Having the Son means having life; not having the Son means not having life. No middle ground.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Water and blood probably refer to Jesus baptism and death. Some false teachers may have separated the heavenly Christ from the earthly Jesus.
The longer reading (Comma Johanneum) adding heavenly witnesses is not original. Early manuscripts lack it; it entered later.

yarbrough.source: Robert Yarbrough, BECNT 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Yarbrough emphasizes the christological focus: the whole passage concerns the identity and work of Jesus. Eternal life is knowing him. Yarbrough traces the epistle's logic through its characteristic contrasts: light and darkness, truth and falsehood, love and hate. The author's binary rhetoric is not simplistic but diagnostic — it exposes the fundamental orientation of the heart and calls readers to genuine self-examination in the light of God's revealed character.

kruse.source: Colin Kruse, TNTC/Pillar 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kruse notes that the three witnesses converge on one truth: Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God. Multiple testimony confirms the gospel. Kruse's analysis focuses on the letter's pastoral function within its historical context. The author addresses concrete community concerns — false teaching, relational fracture, assurance of salvation — with theological precision that grounds pastoral care in the foundational realities of the incarnation and atonement.

hist.context: This is he who came by water and blood - Jesus Christ; not by the water only but by the water and the blood. And the Spirit is the one who testifies, because the Spirit is the truth. For there are three that testify: the Spirit and the water and the blood; and these three agree. If we receive the testimony of men, the testimony of God is greater, for this is the testimony of God that he has borne concerning his Son. Whoever believes in the Son of God has the testimony in himself. Whoever does not believe God has made him a liar, because he has not believed in the testimony that God has borne concerning his Son. And this is the testimony, that God gave us eternal life, and this life is in his Son. Whoever has the Son has life; whoever does not have the Son of God does not have life.

heb.paragraph: I write these things that you may know that you have eternal life - assurance is John purpose. Knowledge is possible.
This is the confidence we have toward him - boldness in prayer flows from relationship. We ask according to his will.
Little children, keep yourselves from idols - the abrupt closing warns against any substitute for the true God.

refs.note: These are written so that you may believe and have life in his name.
Whatever you ask in my name, this I will do.
Whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven - unforgivable sin.

notes.note: I write so that you who believe may know you have eternal life - assurance is the purpose.
Confidence in prayer according to his will. Pray for a brother sinning - God will give life. But there is sin leading to death.
Everyone born of God does not keep on sinning. The Son of God has come and given us understanding. Keep yourselves from idols.
Calvin emphasizes that assurance is God intended gift. Doubt is not humility but failure to receive what God offers.
Sin leading to death is most likely apostasy - deliberate, final rejection of Christ. For such, prayer is not prescribed.
The closing warning seems abrupt but fits the context: anything that takes the place of the true God revealed in Christ is idolatry.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Sin leading to death is debated: apostasy, blasphemy against the Spirit, or sin resulting in physical death. The first fits the context best.
He is the true God - the antecedent could be God the Father or Jesus Christ. Grammar slightly favors Christ, which would be a clear deity claim.

kruse.source: Colin Kruse, TNTC/Pillar 1-3 John — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kruse emphasizes that the closing warnings balance assurance. Confidence is not complacency; vigilance against idols is still required. Kruse's analysis focuses on the letter's pastoral function within its historical context. The author addresses concrete community concerns — false teaching, relational fracture, assurance of salvation — with theological precision that grounds pastoral care in the foundational realities of the incarnation and atonement.

hist.context: I write these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God, that you may know that you have eternal life. And this is the confidence that we have toward him, that if we ask anything according to his will he hears us. And if we know that he hears us in whatever we ask, we know that we have the requests that we have asked of him. If anyone sees his brother committing a sin not leading to death, he shall ask, and God will give him life - to those who commit sins that do not lead to death. There is sin that leads to death; I do not say that one should pray for that. All wrongdoing is sin, but there is sin that does not lead to death. We know that everyone who has been born of God does not keep on sinning, but he who was born of God protects him, and the evil one does not touch him. We know that we are from God, and the whole world lies in the power of the evil one. And we know that the Son of God has come and has given us understanding, so that we may know him who is true; and we are in him who is true, in his Son Jesus Christ. He is the true God and eternal life. Little children, keep yourselves from idols.

themes.note: Faith dominates as the means of victory and assurance. Sovereignty appears in the Son protection and the evil one limitation. The true God is the object of faith and source of life.

rows.label: vv. 6-12
rows.text: The three witnesses; life in the Son

lit.note: Chapter 5 concludes with assurance: faith overcomes, witnesses confirm, and believers can know they have eternal life. The abrupt closing warns against idols.

stories.title: The Comma Johanneum
stories.summary: The Textus Receptus and KJV include a Trinitarian gloss in 1 John 5:7: 'the Father, the Word, and the Holy Spirit, and these three are one.' This is the most explicit Trinitarian statement in the New Testament — but it is entirely absent from Greek manuscripts before the 16th century.

evidence.reading: Absent from every known Greek manuscript
Absent from most; appears only in later Latin MSS
Earliest citation, in a Latin theological treatise
Absent — Erasmus excluded it citing lack of Greek evidence
Present — apparently based on a late Latin-influenced Greek MS
Present — widely suspected of being created to pressure Erasmus

stories.consensus: The Comma Johanneum is universally recognized by textual critics as a scribal insertion originating in Latin theological commentary and entering the Greek text only in the 16th century. It is excluded from all critical editions of the Greek New Testament (NA28, UBS5).
stories.significance: The episode illustrates the difference between the history of Christian doctrine and the textual history of Scripture. Trinitarian theology does not rest on this interpolation; the doctrine is well-attested from genuine texts. The Comma is a lesson in why textual criticism matters.

discourse.title: 1 John 5 — Faith Overcomes the World and Assurance of Eternal Life
discourse.thesis: Faith in Jesus as Son of God overcomes the world. The Spirit, water, and blood testify. We know we have eternal life.

nodes.label: Faith overcomes (5:1-5)
nodes.text: Everyone who believes that Jesus is the Christ has been born of God. Everyone who loves the Father loves whoever has been born of him. Loving God means keeping his commandments, and his commandments are not burdensome. Everyone born of God overcomes the world — our faith is the victory.

nodes.label: Three witnesses (5:6-12)
nodes.text: Jesus came by water and blood — not by water only but by water and blood. The Spirit testifies, because the Spirit is truth. There are three that testify: Spirit, water, and blood, and these three agree. If we receive human testimony, God's testimony is greater: whoever believes in the Son has the testimony in himself. Whoever has the Son has life.

nodes.label: Assurance (5:13-17)
nodes.text: I write these things so that you who believe in the Son may know you have eternal life. This is the confidence: if we ask anything according to his will, he hears us. If anyone sees his brother committing a sin not leading to death, he should ask and God will give life. There is sin that leads to death; I do not say one should pray for that.

nodes.label: Three certainties (5:18-21)
nodes.text: We know that everyone born of God does not keep on sinning — the one born of God protects him. We know that we are from God and the whole world lies in the power of the evil one. We know that the Son of God has come and has given us understanding to know him who is true. Little children, keep yourselves from idols.

discourse.note: The letter ends abruptly with 'keep yourselves from idols' — possibly referencing the secessionists' Christology as idolatrous distortion.

coaching.tip: 'This is the victory that has overcome the world — our faith' (v. 4). Victory is past tense: 'has overcome.' The battle is won. Faith doesn't earn victory but participates in a victory already achieved. 'Who is it that overcomes the world except the one who believes that Jesus is the Son of God?' (v. 5). Christological faith is world-conquering faith.

'I write these things to you who believe... that you may know that you have eternal life' (v. 13). John's purpose: assurance. Eternal life is present possession ('have'), not future hope. The letter ends not with uncertainty but certainty — you can know. Against those who undermine confidence, John offers solid ground.

prayer_prompt: As John writes 'this is the victory that has overcome the world — our faith,' consider asking God whether you are living as someone who has already won, or as someone still anxiously fighting for an uncertain outcome.
